title: CDMG
lean_code_with_comments:
structure CDMG (Node : Type*) [DecidableEq Node] where
  J : Finset Node
  V : Finset Node
  hJV_disj : Disjoint J V
  E : Finset (Node × Node)
  hE_subset : ∀ ⦃e : Node × Node⦄, e ∈ E → e.1 ∈ J ∪ V ∧ e.2 ∈ V
  L : Finset (Node × Node)
  hL_subset : ∀ ⦃e : Node × Node⦄, e ∈ L → e.1 ∈ V ∧ e.2 ∈ V
  hL_irrefl : ∀ ⦃v1 v2 : Node⦄, (v1, v2) ∈ L → v1 ≠ v2
  hL_symm : ∀ ⦃v1 v2 : Node⦄, (v1, v2) ∈ L → (v2, v1) ∈ L
lean_code_without_comments:
structure CDMG (Node : Type*) [DecidableEq Node] where
  J : Finset Node
  V : Finset Node
  hJV_disj : Disjoint J V
  E : Finset (Node × Node)
  hE_subset : ∀ ⦃e : Node × Node⦄, e ∈ E → e.1 ∈ J ∪ V ∧ e.2 ∈ V
  L : Finset (Node × Node)
  hL_subset : ∀ ⦃e : Node × Node⦄, e ∈ L → e.1 ∈ V ∧ e.2 ∈ V
  hL_irrefl : ∀ ⦃v1 v2 : Node⦄, (v1, v2) ∈ L → v1 ≠ v2
  hL_symm : ∀ ⦃v1 v2 : Node⦄, (v1, v2) ∈ L → (v2, v1) ∈ L
lean_explanation:
The Lean encoding bundles the LN's tuple $(J, V, E, L)$ into a single `structure CDMG` parameterised by an ambient node type. The parameter `Node : Type*` is universe-polymorphic and corresponds to the LN's silent assumption that nodes live in some fixed carrier; the `[DecidableEq Node]` instance is the minimal typeclass needed for `Finset` to store nodes and edges and to decide equality of pairs in the kernel.

The four data fields mirror the LN one-for-one. `J : Finset Node` and `V : Finset Node` are the input and output node sets; using `Finset` rather than `Set` makes their finiteness (per the operator clarification `[manual_1]`) computable and reusable downstream. `E : Finset (Node × Node)` is the set of directed edges and `L : Finset (Node × Node)` is the set of bidirected edges, both encoded as finite sets of ordered pairs so they share the same Mathlib `Finset (Node × Node)` API.

The five proof fields carry the LN's constraints. `hJV_disj : Disjoint J V` uses Mathlib's order-theoretic `Disjoint` predicate (here unfolding to `J ∩ V = ∅`) to enforce the LN's parenthetical "(disjoint)" on the vertex sets. The membership constraints `hE_subset` and `hL_subset` realise $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ and $L \subseteq V \times V$; both quantify over pairs using strict-implicit binders `⦃e : Node × Node⦄`, which Lean fills in only once the membership hypothesis pins down the pair, keeping call sites uncluttered. The remaining two fields `hL_irrefl` and `hL_symm` encode irreflexivity ($v_1 \ne v_2$) and symmetry ($(v_1,v_2) \in L \iff (v_2,v_1) \in L$) of the bidirected relation, jointly replacing the LN's quotient $V \times V / ((v_1,v_2) \sim (v_2,v_1))$. The structure exposes its four data fields and five proof fields under the names `G.J`, `G.V`, `G.E`, `G.L`, `G.hJV_disj`, …, which is how every later definition in chapter 3 destructures a CDMG.
design_choices:
**A bespoke `structure`, not a Mathlib graph type or a typeclass.** A CDMG is a *piece of data* the rest of the chapter destructures, not a property to be inferred, so `class` would be wrong. None of Mathlib's graph types fits either: `SimpleGraph` is undirected and has no input/output split, and `Quiver` carries ordered edges but no bidirected channel and no $J/V$ partition. A custom `structure` is the only encoding that simultaneously holds the $J$-vs-$V$ partition, the directed channel $E$, and the bidirected channel $L$ in one record.

**A single ambient `Node : Type*`, not a two-sort encoding.** A natural alternative would be to take input and output nodes as separate types `J V : Type*`. We reject it because the LN immediately treats $J \cup V$ as a single ambient set: walks, ancestor sets, topological orders and marginalisation all quantify uniformly over $J \cup V$. A two-sort encoding would force a `Sum` coproduct and pervasive coercions at every use site. Keeping a single `Node` together with the proof field `hJV_disj : Disjoint J V` recovers the partition where needed while leaving $J \cup V$ as a plain `Finset Node`.

**Ordered pairs with explicit symmetry for `L`, not Lean's quotient.** The LN writes $L$ as a quotient $V \times V / ((v_1,v_2) \sim (v_2,v_1))$, but the operator clarified that ordered-pair-plus-symmetry is equivalent. We adopt the latter so that `L` shares its carrier `Finset (Node × Node)` with `E` and avoids a `Quot.mk` / `Quot.lift` dance at every destructuring site. The visible cost — flagged here for downstream rows — is that each undirected bidirected edge appears *twice* in `L`, once in each orientation; consumers that count or enumerate bidirected edges must either divide by two or pick a canonical orientation.

**Membership constraints as stand-alone proof fields, not subtype carriers.** A purist alternative would be to type $E$ as a `Finset` over the subtype $(J \cup V) \times V$. We instead carry `E : Finset (Node × Node)` together with `hE_subset` (and analogously for `L`) so that every consumer can destructure edges as plain pairs and invoke or rewrite the subset constraint independently, with no subtype coercion at the use site.

**No `E ∩ L = ∅` field, and self-loops in `E` are admitted.** The literal LN imposes no $E$-irreflexivity and the operator reads its "(disjoint)" qualifier on the edge sets as type-level only — so the same ordered pair $(v,w)$ may sit in both $E$ and $L$, and a directed self-loop $(v,v) \in E$ is legal. Likewise the LN's would-be nonemptiness constraint $J \cup V \ne \emptyset$ is commented out, so the empty CDMG is a legal inhabitant. These choices follow the literal LN; downstream definitions that need a stricter form add the constraint at the use site.
